(30) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Tricks: Financial Freedom
This one's mostly chit-chat — the title is tongue-in-cheek, but writing a blog can actually make a tiny bit of money.
Can you really make money writing a blog?

You can make some money writing a blog, but reaching financial freedom from it? That's a stretch, unless you write incredibly well. Honestly the money is pretty small — for me, maybe under NT$3000 a year (though I'm sure I'm just on the low end).
How does blog monetisation actually work? Usually you embed ads in your posts — Google AdSense being the classic. Personally I put ads in the sidebar, plus at the start and end of articles:
Another option is LikeCoin — similar concept to Medium's clap mechanism. I'll cover both.
For the application requirements, I covered them in an earlier post
Adding Google AdSense to Hexo NexT (article in Mandarin)
. I'll just paste the relevant points here:
Your site can't be a content farm (i.e. junk articles)
Your site has to have been live at least six months (one of my other sites took over 7 months to get approved)
Content has to be substantive and over a certain volume (my other site had over 60 posts when it got approved)
The application criteria honestly aren't that clear. I've applied three times, all with different outcomes. The only thing that's certain is the 18+ age requirement.

Google AdSense pays in two ways: impressions and clicks. Click revenue is when a user actually clicks an ad. Impression revenue is when an ad just shows up in the user's browser — but impression revenue is much lower than click revenue, unless your traffic is high enough to overtake it.

One thing that's strictly forbidden — telling users to click your AdSense ads. You'd earn more, sure, but you can't write things like "if you liked this post, click the ad..." or anything that encourages users to click. That's against the rules. Same goes for clicking your own ads. If you get caught, your account gets permanently frozen with no way to reapply. So really, don't.
One more thing about impressions: AdSense has a big issue — if a user has AdBlock installed, your ad isn't counted as an impression.
If you're interested in AdSense and want to try it out, you can refer to my older post
. Some of the details may differ a bit since NexT has updated a lot since then, but the concepts are the same.
LikeCoin is a "likes-as-rewards" concept, similar to Medium's clap mechanism. What makes
interesting is that it's a token built on Ethereum, so it's decentralised.

Once you've accumulated enough likes, you can convert them into fiat currency (i.e. real money). LikeCoin supports a few sign-up methods, including the usual Google and Facebook login, so registering isn't difficult.
Adding LikeCoin to your blog isn't hard either — the official docs cover it, and the guide happens to be NexT-specific:
How to add a LikeCoin button in Hexo (Mandarin)
language-swig shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Not the first version of the snippet shown in the docs.
Once it's in, you'll see the like button on your site:
If you're interested in LikeCoin, you can also use my referral link to install their app.
This post was mostly chit-chat — reaching financial freedom through blogging is a pretty wild claim. But picking up a little passive income from your articles isn't bad at all.
This was technically the last Ironman post, but I've prepared an extra 31st post as a wrap-up — mainly to cover some common Hexo issues.
